title: What will it take to accelerate global Small Modular Reactor (SMR) deployment
text:
In our first post of the series, we discussed the role of nuclear power in the energy transition and outlined common risks faced by new nuclear builds.
Nuclear power build-out alongside renewables is key to meeting energy transition goals. There are several benefits of nuclear energy which compliment renewables, including: providing baseload power, dispatchability for load following, long operating lifetimes and high capacity factors.
At COP28 in December 2023, over 20 countries declared a commitment to triple nuclear capacity. To achieve this, a yearly increase of 4-5 times the current pace of nuclear new builds will be required. Alongside the need for new large-scale nuclear builds, SMRs are uniquely suited to help match electricity demand and bolster the nuclear industry. SMRs share similar technology to their large-scale counterparts but provide <500 MW power outputs (vs. 1 GW). They are constructed from factory-made assemblies and offer advanced safety systems.
Despite its benefits, building new nuclear capacity necessitates large-scale implementation with significant cost and scheduling risks. As such, de-risking SMR projects is key to ensuring its role in the clean-energy future. To accelerate SMR deployment and ensure successful execution, these risks need to be addressed. Here, we list the most effective mitigations for each of the five risks previously identified.
1. Project execution: delivering projects on-time and withinbudget
Complete design and planning phases before construction begins. Design for critical path construction activities should be complete prior to ‘shovels in the ground’ to avoid unnecessary rework and delays in the field. It is important to involve contractors and construction trades early in the design and planning phases to ensure simplicity, constructability, and the integration of efficiencies from previous projects.
Invest early in project teams. Commit a quarter of upfront funding to project planning and workforce development, including mobilizing teams, training, engaging vendors, and developing preliminary budgets and schedules. This ensures the team is prepared to execute the project from the onset – especially for FOAK (first-of-a-kind) deployments, as discussed below.
Implement project controls pre-construction. Project controls teams should be independent of project management teams and serve as auditors external to the project team by providing ongoing auditing of key metrics across projects and sites.
Commit to a robust contracting strategy. Adopt contracting principles and structures that align stakeholder incentives and promote unified project goals through mutual risk and reward sharing. The contract should ensure sufficient risk-sharing to avoid financial insolvencies in the face of potential project delays or budget overruns (e.g., Integrated Project Delivery model for initial SMR deployments to foster collaboration between execution partners – EPC6, OEM7, owners).
2. Cost and learning curve efficiencies: realizing benefits from first-of-a-kind (FOAK) to nth-of-a-kind (NOAK)
Government support for FOAK. Governments have most to gain as ultimate guarantors for the complete energy system. As such, they should invest in first units to reduce project execution partners’ risk while private capital watches uncertainty decrease. These investments include overrun insurance and grant funding for FOAK projects. For example, FOAK funding has been implemented by the Canada Infrastructure Bank (CIB) which committed $970M CAD for Phase 1 of the Darlington New Nuclear Project to build GE Hitachi’s BWRX-300 SMR by the late 2020s. This insurance is crucial until there is proven execution path for new builds. Implementing the other mitigations outlined here will limit the need for this contingency.
Standardization in design. Use a standard plant design across projects and effectively manage changes for design integrity, consistency, and cost savings. Implement changes in ‘flights’ (e.g., ~10 units at a time) to limit design iterations and enable repeatability across units. This approach has been successful in the French civil (‘flight’ one with 18 identical reactors and ‘flight’ two with 10 identical reactors) and US submarine fleets. Change management can be coordinated through organized groups where owners and OEMs collaborate to make unified decisions on design improvements. Alignment of standard design and licensing across countries can further drive cost and learning efficiencies. In line with this thinking, US, Canada and UK regulatory bodies have recently committed to streamline reviews and share best practices.
Stable clients and volume commitment. Learning curve efficiencies are directly proportional to the number of units being built. Owners deploying fleets of SMRs (3+ units), limited to one or two technologies (at first), will kickstart the whole nuclear value chain: supply chain, fuel, workforce, and beyond, allowing for synergies across builds. See mitigation #3 for enablers on how to unlock required volumes.
Share lessons learned across builds. Leverage the nuclear power sector’s culture of openness and collective sharing. Establish a mechanism that requires owners, execution partners, and OEMs to share honest lessons (not just marketing materials) across builds and sites. This includes leveraging general nuclear project insights (including past construction lessons) from established industry groups and tech-specific learnings from owners who utilize the same designs.
Implementing these mitigation strategies can result in learning curve rates of about 30% between the first and fourth unit, resulting in significant cost savings as more are built (Figure 1).
Figure 1: Expected learning curve rates between Unit 1 and Unit 4
Industry benchmarks: DOE Liftoff Report for Advanced Nuclear anticipates a reactor-to-reactor learning rate between 12-15%, consistent with South Korea, NPP builds over ~40-year period and French multi-unit builds.
3. Revenue uncertainty: securing revenue for owners
Pass government support for clean energy procurement. Government commitment to purchase carbon-free baseload electricity from nuclear generators provides revenue certainty and potential cost savings to the owner.
Develop incentive schemes. Governments can incentivize nuclear energy generation through programs like the US IRA’s Investment and Production Tax Credits (ITC and PTC). These programs significantly reduce the cost of power generation for the owner.
Encourage long-term contracts. Offtake contracts like PPAs can provide owners with up to 25 years of stable revenue and avoid the volatility of electricity markets. This can help secure financing, fund recurring maintenance and refurbishment costs and allow the plant to operate successfully.
4. Workforce and development: shortage of labor with nuclear power experience
Fund workforce development. Establish grant funding for workforce development and training programs targeted at junior- to mid-level staff, including scholarships, apprenticeships, and educational outreach. These should also educate workers about competitive market rates to incentivize long-term careers in the nuclear industry. For example, the US DOE is encouraging nuclear workforce development through the University Nuclear Leadership Program (UNLP), which will award up to $10k a year per student in scholarships and fellowships for people pursuing degrees in nuclear energy and engineering. Incorporate technology like GenAI to enhance training programs and accelerate learnings.
Engage early with prospective workers. Execution partners should engage educational institutions like universities and trade schools early to signal demand and spur interest in the nuclear industry. Labor unions also play an important role in upskilling members and connecting workers with resources needed to succeed in the industry. For the initial units, contractors and owners should tap into international networks to engage specialists and engineers with new build experience.
Incorporate military nuclear personnel into the workforce planning strategy. Programs like 'Helmets to Hardhats', which facilitate the transition of retiring military personnel for civilian work in the construction industry, serve as excellent models. Leveraging the specialized skills and discipline of these individuals can significantly enhance the workforce, thereby improving both execution and operational excellence in deploying SMRs.
Seek to engage women and promote diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI). Actively engage women and advance DEI in the nuclear industry, which is currently perceived as highly male-dominated. This perception not only narrows the talent pool but also overlooks a significant portion of potential contributors. By offering targeted incentives and promoting the sector among women and underrepresented groups, we can expand the talent base, thereby increasing capacity and innovation in the deployment of SMRs.
5. Nuclear safety: public perception of nuclear power
Educate the public on the benefits of nuclear. Engage with community groups to explain the technology behind nuclear power and highlight the vital necessity of including nuclear in the energy mix. Key messages include minimal pollution and a low death rate from accidents, similar to solar PV, wind and hydropower. It is also important to highlight the need for nuclear energy when solar and wind resources are not available – its role as a complement to renewables, not a replacement.
Communicate the enhanced safety of new reactors. Effectively communicating the advanced safety systems of new reactor designs can instill public confidence in the technology. It is crucial to inform the public that new reactor designs, especially SMRs, include passive safety systems to safely shut down the reactor in case of emergencies, reducing the reliance on active safety systems and operator intervention. Additionally, simpler designs limit the number components and potential points of failure.
There is a clear and urgent need to ensure timely deployment of nuclear power to meet global clean energy goals and electricity demand. Successful deployment has the potential to be a game-changer for the global nuclear industry and for countries trying to meet their decarbonization commitments. But to realize the benefits of nuclear power, it is critical to de-risk deployment through government support, financial incentives, and collaboration between project partners. Implementing these mitigations can help kickstart the next generation of nuclear power.
************
Appendix: Technology readiness and competitive landscape
There are several SMR designs in development, with different power and temperature capacities, technologies, and vendor offerings. Reactor designs will have different frequencies depending on country of origin (60 hertz in USA vs. 50 hertz in EU and UK), which means that pumps and other equipment should be adapted to meet the frequency of the deployment country. Additionally, newer technologies use different coolants (e.g., molten salt, sodium, liquid metal) and fuel types (e.g., high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU)), compared to conventional reactors which use water as a coolant and low-enriched uranium (LEU) as a fuel source.
Below, we list a sub-set of SMR designs and their details. This list is based on reactors in the NEA SMR Dashboard that are ranked with a high confidence of deployment based on licensing status, project siting, secured financing, supply chain readiness, engagement with communities, and fuel availability.
BCG has worked with public and private clients globally to optimize their current nuclear portfolios and go-forward strategies for both conventional and advanced technologies. Our work spans the entire nuclear value chain from fuel and supply chain to project implementation, operations, and decommissioning.
Nice job BCGers. Idaho National Laboratory director John Wagner just published a similar piece on the need for deployment
speed in Nuclear News, but in several important areas you went into even more depth. So much depends on SMR success. Appreciation.
